clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is brought by his parents who are concerned he has not spoken a single word yet. He does not respond when his name is called, does not point at objects to share interest, and avoids eye contact. He lines up his toy cars in a specific order and becomes extremely distressed if they are moved. He has no medical history of note and hearing tests are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This child demonstrates the core DSM-5 features of Autism Spectrum Disorder: persistent deficits in social communication (no speech, no response to name, no joint attention, avoids eye contact) AND restricted/repetitive behaviors (lining up toys in a specific order, extreme distress at change). Onset is clearly before age 3. Normal hearing rules out sensory hearing loss as a cause of speech delay.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Intellectual Disability causes global developmental delay but does not specifically produce the pattern of restricted/repetitive behaviors and social communication deficits seen here. ID can co-occur with ASD but is not the primary diagnosis.
Correct. ASD is diagnosed by deficits in social communication PLUS restricted/repetitive behaviors present from early childhood, which fits this vignette perfectly.
Specific Language Disorder affects language alone; social interaction and nonverbal communication remain intact. This child has social deficits beyond language.
Reactive Attachment Disorder follows documented early neglect or institutional care. There is no such history here, and RAD does not produce repetitive behaviors.
ADHD presents with hyperactivity, impulsivity, and inattention — not social communication deficits or restricted/repetitive behaviors as the primary features.
clinicalPearl: ASD requires BOTH domains: (1) social communication deficits AND (2) restricted/repetitive behaviors. Either domain alone is insufficient for the diagnosis.
keyClue: No speech + no response to name + avoids eye contact (social domain) AND lining up toys + distress at change (repetitive domain) = ASD.
commonExamTrap: Assuming speech delay alone = ASD. Specific Language Disorder also causes speech delay but lacks the social and repetitive features.
missedConcept: Joint attention (pointing, showing objects to share interest) is a key social communication milestone — its absence is an early red flag for ASD.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is referred for behavioral difficulties at school. He has an extensive knowledge of dinosaur taxonomy and insists on discussing this topic with peers who are uninterested. He struggles to understand social cues and has no close friends despite wanting them. His teachers note he takes instructions very literally. His language development was normal — he spoke his first words at 12 months and had normal grammar by age 2. His IQ is 115. Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
options: Autism Spectrum Disorder with intellectual disability
Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder, inattentive type
explanation: Asperger Syndrome presents with the social communication deficits of ASD (poor social cues, literal interpretation, difficulty making friends, one-sided conversations about circumscribed interests) but WITHOUT language delay and WITHOUT intellectual disability. This child had normal language milestones and above-average IQ — distinguishing features from classic ASD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ASD with intellectual disability would require cognitive impairment (IQ < 70) and typically presents with language delay. This child has normal language and IQ 115.
Correct. Asperger Syndrome = ASD features (social deficits, restricted interests) + normal language development + average or above-average IQ.
Conduct Disorder involves rule-breaking, aggression, and violation of others' rights — none of which are described here.
Social Anxiety Disorder involves fear-based avoidance of social situations. This child wants friends (no avoidance drive) but lacks the skills to make them — a social competence deficit, not anxiety.
ADHD inattentive type causes attention and organizational problems, not circumscribed interests or literal interpretation of language.
clinicalPearl: The key distinguishing feature of Asperger Syndrome from classic ASD is preserved early language development and average-to-high IQ. Both share social communication deficits and restricted/repetitive behaviors.
keyClue: Normal language milestones + high IQ + social deficits + circumscribed intense interest = Asperger Syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Confusing Asperger Syndrome with classic ASD. In ASD there is typically language delay; Asperger has normal early language.
missedConcept: Children with Asperger Syndrome often want social relationships but lack the intuitive social skills to maintain them — unlike social anxiety where the drive to connect may itself be diminished.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy with a confirmed diagnosis of ADHD has been started on methylphenidate. After 4 weeks, his parents report he has significant insomnia and reduced appetite. His symptoms of inattention and hyperactivity have improved modestly. His pediatrician wants to switch to a non-stimulant medication that acts via a different mechanism. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative?
explanation: Atomoxetine (Strattera) is a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — the first non-stimulant approved for ADHD. It does not carry the insomnia and appetite suppression side effects of stimulants. It is the drug of choice when stimulants are not tolerated or are contraindicated (e.g., history of substance abuse or tic disorders).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amphetamine salts are another stimulant — they would likely reproduce or worsen the same side effects (insomnia, appetite loss) as methylphenidate.
Correct. Atomoxetine is a selective NE reuptake inhibitor (non-stimulant) FDA-approved for ADHD with a different side-effect profile from stimulants.
Sertraline is an SSRI used for depression, anxiety, and OCD. It has no approved indication for ADHD.

Clonidine (alpha-2 agonist) can be used as adjunctive therapy in ADHD, particularly for tics or aggression, but is not the first-line non-stimulant alternative to methylphenidate for core ADHD symptoms.
Bupropion is used for depression and smoking cessation. It lowers the seizure threshold and is not approved for paediatric ADHD.
clinicalPearl: Atomoxetine is the first-line non-stimulant for ADHD. Mechanism: selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor. Takes 4-6 weeks for full effect. Does not cause insomnia or appetite suppression unlike stimulants.
keyClue: Non-stimulant + selective NE reuptake inhibitor + ADHD = Atomoxetine (Strattera).
commonExamTrap: Choosing amphetamines as an alternative to methylphenidate — both are stimulants with similar side effect profiles.
missedConcept: Atomoxetine's mechanism (NE reuptake inhibition) is fundamentally different from stimulants (dopamine/NE release or reuptake inhibition). This is why its side-effect profile differs.

clinicalStem: A mother brings her 6-year-old daughter to the clinic complaining that the child wets the bed at least 3 nights per week. The child has never had a dry night. Physical examination and urinalysis are normal. The pediatrician confirms a diagnosis of primary nocturnal enuresis. The family wants pharmacological treatment because behavioral strategies have failed. Which medication is most appropriate?
explanation: Desmopressin (synthetic ADH analog) is the first-line pharmacological treatment for nocturnal enuresis. It reduces urine production overnight. It is preferred over imipramine for its superior safety profile. Imipramine (a TCA) is a second-line alternative but carries cardiac and overdose risks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Imipramine (TCA) is a second-line option for enuresis. It works via anticholinergic effects reducing bladder contractions + central ADH-like effect. It is less preferred due to cardiac arrhythmia risk and toxicity in overdose.
Correct. Desmopressin is the first-line drug for nocturnal enuresis — it reduces overnight urine output via V2 receptor agonism.
Oxybutynin is an anticholinergic used for daytime urge incontinence (overactive bladder) — not the standard for nocturnal enuresis.
Furosemide is a loop diuretic that would dramatically worsen enuresis by increasing urine output.
Methylphenidate is a stimulant for ADHD with no role in treating enuresis.
clinicalPearl: DSM-5 enuresis: involuntary voiding ≥2x/week for ≥3 months in a child ≥5 years. Primary = never achieved dryness. First-line pharma: desmopressin. Second-line: imipramine (TCA).
keyClue: Age ≥5 + bed-wetting ≥2x/week + normal urinalysis + behavioral therapy failed = desmopressin.
commonExamTrap: Choosing imipramine as first-line. Desmopressin is first-line due to better safety. Imipramine is reserved for when desmopressin fails.
missedConcept: Primary enuresis = never been dry. Secondary enuresis = relapse after ≥6 months of dryness (often triggered by stress).

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old girl with known anorexia nervosa is brought to the emergency department by her parents. She has been restricting food intake for 6 months. Examination reveals: weight 38 kg (height 162 cm), HR 44 bpm (awake), BP 88/60 mmHg with orthostatic drop, temperature 35.4°C. She denies suicidal ideation. Which of the following findings is the most compelling indication for immediate inpatient hospitalization?
options: Heart rate of 44 bpm while awake
Presence of anorexia nervosa diagnosis alone
Denial of the severity of illness
explanation: Bradycardia with HR < 50 bpm while awake is a medical hospitalization criterion for anorexia nervosa. This patient's HR of 44 bpm meets this threshold, combined with hypothermia (< 36°C), orthostatic hypotension, and low body weight. Bradycardia reflects cardiac compromise and risk of life-threatening arrhythmia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: BMI < 17.5 is a DSM-IV threshold and used in severity grading, but the specific hospitalization criterion is < 75% of ideal body weight. BMI alone, while concerning, is not the most compelling single indication compared to active cardiac compromise.
Correct. HR < 50 bpm awake is a direct hospitalization criterion for AN. It reflects cardiac dysfunction and risk of fatal arrhythmia.
AN diagnosis alone does not mandate hospitalization — most patients are managed as outpatients unless medical instability is present.
Duration of illness indicates chronicity but is not a hospitalization criterion in itself.
Denial of illness severity (anosognosia) is characteristic of AN but is not a medical hospitalization trigger on its own.
clinicalPearl: Medical hospitalization criteria for AN: HR < 50 awake or < 45 asleep, hypothermia < 36°C, orthostatic hypotension, weight < 75% ideal body weight, dehydration, hypokalemia, severe depression/suicidality.
keyClue: HR 44 bpm awake = bradycardia below the < 50 threshold = immediate hospitalization required.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the low BMI alone rather than recognizing that active physiological decompensation (bradycardia, hypothermia, orthostasis) is the strongest hospitalization indicator.
missedConcept: In AN, cardiac complications are the leading cause of death. Bradycardia signals myocardial dysfunction from starvation-induced muscle wasting.
redFlagReason: Bradycardia HR 44 bpm + hypothermia + orthostatic hypotension in AN = medical emergency with high risk of fatal arrhythmia requiring immediate hospitalization.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old girl with severe anorexia nervosa (BMI 13.5) is admitted to hospital and begins nutritional rehabilitation. On day 3 of refeeding, she develops generalized muscle weakness, tetany, and cardiac arrhythmias. Her ECG shows prolonged QT interval. Investigations reveal: phosphate 0.4 mmol/L (low), potassium 2.9 mmol/L (low), magnesium 0.5 mmol/L (low). Which of the following best explains this clinical picture?
explanation: Refeeding syndrome occurs when malnourished patients receive carbohydrates after prolonged starvation. Insulin surge drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium from the extracellular to intracellular space, causing severe hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia. These electrolyte shifts lead to cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, tetany, and potentially death. Onset is typically 2-4 days after refeeding begins.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hyponatremia causes neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures) rather than the specific triad of hypophosphatemia + hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia with cardiac arrhythmia.
Correct. Refeeding syndrome = carbohydrate intake → insulin surge → intracellular shift of phosphate/K+/Mg2+ → hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, cardiac and neuromuscular complications.
Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency) presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia — not the electrolyte pattern described here.
Addisonian crisis causes hyponatremia and hyperkalemia — the opposite electrolyte picture.
Hyperthyroidism does not cause the specific electrolyte disturbances or the clinical context described (refeeding after starvation).
clinicalPearl: Refeeding syndrome: prevention requires slow refeeding with electrolyte monitoring and prophylactic phosphate/thiamine supplementation. Risk is highest in BMI < 14, minimal intake for > 5 days, or recent weight loss > 1 kg/week.
keyClue: Refeeding after starvation + day 2-4 + hypophosphatemia + hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia + cardiac arrhythmia = refeeding syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing simple hypokalemia rather than recognizing the full refeeding syndrome picture. The combination of hypophosphatemia (key marker), hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia together in the refeeding context is diagnostic.
missedConcept: Hypophosphatemia is the hallmark electrolyte disturbance in refeeding syndrome. Phosphate is critical for ATP production — its depletion impairs all energy-dependent processes.
redFlagReason: Refeeding syndrome can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Recognition and electrolyte correction in the first 72 hours of refeeding is critical.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents for a routine check-up. She is of normal weight. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement, erosion of dental enamel on the lingual surfaces, and calluses on the dorsum of her right hand at the knuckle level. She is evasive when asked about her eating habits but denies restricting food. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation is classic for bulimia nervosa. The triad of parotid enlargement (from repeated vomiting stimulation), dental enamel erosion on lingual surfaces (from chronic acid exposure), and Russell's sign (calluses on the dorsum of the hand from repeated contact with teeth during self-induced vomiting) are pathognomonic physical findings. Normal body weight distinguishes BN from AN.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anorexia nervosa binge-purge subtype would also present with low body weight. This patient is of normal weight, making BN far more likely.
Correct. Parotid enlargement + dental enamel erosion + Russell's sign in a normal-weight patient = bulimia nervosa until proven otherwise.
Binge eating disorder involves binge eating WITHOUT compensatory purging behaviors. There would be no parotid enlargement, no dental enamel erosion, and no Russell's sign.
Sjogren's syndrome causes parotid enlargement and dry mouth due to autoimmune destruction of salivary glands — not calluses on the dorsum of the hand or dental enamel erosion from acid.
GERD could theoretically cause some dental erosion, but it would not explain parotid enlargement or Russell's sign.
clinicalPearl: Russell's sign = calluses on the dorsum of the hand (knuckles) from repeated contact with teeth during self-induced vomiting. Named after Gerald Russell who first described it.
keyClue: Russell's sign + parotid enlargement + dental enamel erosion + normal weight = bulimia nervosa.
commonExamTrap: Missing the normal body weight — a key distinguishing feature. BN patients are often normal weight or slightly overweight, unlike AN patients.
missedConcept: Physical examination can reveal BN even when the patient denies purging behaviors. Russell's sign is a specific examination finding for self-induced vomiting.

clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman with a 3-year history of bulimia nervosa is also a heavy smoker and requests help with smoking cessation. Her psychiatrist prescribes a medication that will help both her bulimia and smoking cessation. Which of the following medications is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with bulimia nervosa?
explanation: Bupropion is absolutely contraindicated in bulimia nervosa. It lowers the seizure threshold, and patients with BN are at significantly elevated risk of seizures due to electrolyte disturbances (particularly hypokalemia from purging). The combination creates a dangerous risk of seizures. Bupropion is used for depression and smoking cessation but must not be used in eating disorder patients with purging behaviors.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fluoxetine is the only FDA-approved pharmacological treatment for BN. It is the preferred SSRI and reduces binge-purge frequency. Not contraindicated.
Correct. Bupropion is CONTRAINDICATED in BN. It lowers seizure threshold; BN patients have electrolyte disturbances that also lower seizure threshold — the combination is dangerous.
Sertraline (SSRI) has good evidence for BN treatment. It is not contraindicated.
CBT is the gold-standard non-pharmacological treatment for BN. It is not a medication and has no contraindication here.
Topiramate (anticonvulsant) has some evidence for reducing binge-purge frequency in BN and is not contraindicated.
clinicalPearl: Bupropion is CONTRAINDICATED in BN (and AN). Mechanism of risk: bupropion lowers seizure threshold + eating disorder electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia) also lower seizure threshold = high seizure risk.
keyClue: Eating disorder + smoking cessation request = DO NOT give bupropion. Give nicotine replacement therapy instead.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing bupropion for smoking cessation or depression without checking for eating disorder history — a dangerous clinical error.
missedConcept: Fluoxetine is the only FDA-approved drug for bulimia nervosa. CBT + fluoxetine is the evidence-based first-line treatment.
redFlagReason: Prescribing bupropion to a BN patient risks seizures due to combined seizure threshold lowering from the drug and electrolyte disturbances from purging.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents reporting episodes of eating very large amounts of food in a discrete period, feeling out of control during these episodes, which occur at least twice weekly for the past 4 months. She feels significant guilt and shame after these episodes. She denies vomiting, using laxatives, or excessive exercising after eating. She is obese (BMI 33). Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
explanation: Binge eating disorder (BED) is characterized by recurrent binge eating episodes (large amounts + loss of control) occurring ≥once/week for ≥3 months, with distress (guilt, shame, disgust) — but CRITICALLY without any compensatory behaviors (no purging, no excessive exercise, no fasting). The absence of compensatory behaviors is the key distinction from bulimia nervosa. Obesity is common in BED but weight/appearance concerns are not a diagnostic criterion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bulimia nervosa purging type requires compensatory purging behaviors (vomiting, laxatives, diuretics). This patient explicitly denies these.
Bulimia nervosa non-purging type involves compensatory non-purging behaviors like excessive exercise or fasting. This patient denies any compensatory behavior.
Correct. BED = recurrent binges ≥1x/week for ≥3 months + distress + NO compensatory behaviors. This distinguishes BED from both types of BN.
Night eating syndrome involves eating most calories after dinner or during nighttime awakenings — the timing is characteristic, not described here.
AN binge-purge subtype requires low body weight (BMI significantly below normal). This patient has BMI 33 (obese), which excludes AN.
clinicalPearl: BED vs BN: the single distinguishing feature is compensatory behaviors. BN has them (purging, fasting, excessive exercise). BED does not. Both have binge eating episodes with distress.
keyClue: Binge eating + loss of control + guilt/shame + NO compensatory behaviors = Binge Eating Disorder.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing BN non-purging type when no compensatory behaviors are present. BN always requires some form of compensatory behavior.
missedConcept: Weight and appearance concerns are NOT diagnostic criteria for BED (unlike AN and BN). BED is diagnosed based on binge behavior and distress, not weight.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male presents with a 1-year history of irresistible daytime sleep attacks occurring multiple times daily. He reports that whenever he laughs or feels surprised, his legs suddenly give way and he falls. He also describes vivid dream-like hallucinations as he falls asleep. Overnight polysomnography is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT) and CSF hypocretin/orexin level
explanation: This presentation is classic narcolepsy type 1: excessive daytime sleepiness (sleep attacks) + cataplexy (sudden muscle weakness triggered by strong emotion — laughter, surprise) + hypnagogic hallucinations. Narcolepsy type 1 is caused by autoimmune destruction of hypothalamic hypocretin (orexin)-producing neurons. Confirmation requires: MSLT showing mean sleep latency < 8 min with ≥2 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs), OR CSF hypocretin level ≤ 110 pg/mL (or < 1/3 of mean control values).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Brain MRI may show hypothalamic abnormalities in some cases but is not the confirmatory investigation for narcolepsy. It does not measure hypocretin levels.
Correct. MSLT confirms narcolepsy (short sleep latency + SOREMPs) and CSF hypocretin directly confirms type 1 (low/absent hypocretin from orexin neuron loss).
24-hour EEG is used for seizure disorders. Narcolepsy is not an epileptic condition — sleep attacks are not seizures.
Thyroid function tests are appropriate for fatigue and daytime somnolence screening, but they would not diagnose narcolepsy or explain cataplexy.
Serum ferritin tests for iron deficiency, which can cause restless legs syndrome — a different sleep disorder with no cataplexy.
clinicalPearl: Narcolepsy type 1 = cataplexy + low CSF hypocretin. Type 2 = no cataplexy + normal hypocretin. Mechanism: autoimmune destruction of hypothalamic orexin neurons (associated with HLA-DQB1*06:02).
keyClue: Sleep attacks + cataplexy (emotion-triggered muscle weakness) + hypnagogic hallucinations = narcolepsy type 1. Confirm with MSLT + CSF hypocretin.
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI or EEG instead of MSLT. Narcolepsy is a specific sleep-wake disorder diagnosed by sleep study and CSF analysis — not neuroimaging or EEG.
missedConcept: Cataplexy is pathognomonic for narcolepsy type 1. It is triggered by strong positive emotions (laughter, joy, surprise) — not just any stress. It does NOT occur in hypersomnolence disorder or type 2 narcolepsy.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man is brought by his wife who reports that for the past year he has been shouting, swearing, and violently thrashing his arms during the night. On one occasion he punched her while she was sleeping next to him. He reports vivid dreams of being chased or attacked. He has no history of sleepwalking. Neurological examination is normal. Polysomnography shows absence of normal REM muscle atonia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD) is characterized by acting out vivid, often violent dreams during REM sleep due to the absence of normal REM atonia. Patients physically act out dream content (punching, shouting, kicking). It occurs in older males, during the second half of the night (when REM is predominant), and PSG shows absent REM atonia — the diagnostic finding. RBD is associated with synucleinopathies (Parkinson's disease, Lewy body dementia) — often precedes neurodegeneration by years.
wrongAnswerExplanations: NREM sleepwalking involves complex ambulation during NREM sleep (stage 3). Patients are not acting out dream content and do not recall vivid dreams. Occurs in the first third of the night.
Night terrors occur in NREM sleep in children — involving screaming, autonomic arousal, and amnesia for the event. The patient here has dream recall and is an adult male.
Correct. RBD = absent REM atonia + acting out violent dreams + typically older males + PSG confirmation. Associated with Parkinson's and Lewy body dementia.
Nightmare disorder involves distressing dreams with awakening and recall but WITHOUT physical enactment — the patient wakes up, does not act out the dream violently.
Nocturnal epilepsy can mimic parasomnia but would show epileptiform activity on EEG. PSG here shows absent REM atonia specifically, not seizure activity.
clinicalPearl: RBD in an older male is a strong prodromal marker for Parkinson's disease and Lewy body dementia. Up to 80% of RBD patients develop a synucleinopathy within 10-15 years. Key PSG finding: absent REM atonia.
keyClue: Older male + violent dream enactment + vivid dreams recalled + absent REM atonia on PSG = REM Sleep Behavior Disorder.
commonExamTrap: Confusing RBD with sleepwalking. RBD occurs in REM sleep (second half of night, dream content acted out, patient recalls vivid dreams). Sleepwalking is NREM, first half of night, no dream content.
missedConcept: The clinical importance of RBD extends beyond sleep — it is an early neurodegenerative biomarker. Patients should be counseled and monitored for parkinsonism.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate 900 mg/day for 2 years with a therapeutic level of 0.8 mEq/L. He presents to his GP with a 3-day history of coarse tremor, confusion, ataxia, and nausea. Two weeks ago he was started on ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for knee arthritis. His serum lithium level is now 2.1 mEq/L. What is the best immediate investigation to guide further management?
options: Repeat serum lithium level with renal function tests and ECG
explanation: Lithium has the narrowest therapeutic index of all mood stabilizers. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing GFR and lithium excretion — causing lithium levels to rise into toxic range (> 1.5 mEq/L). Level of 2.1 mEq/L with symptoms (coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion = signs of moderate-severe toxicity) requires urgent repeat lithium level, renal function (creatinine/eGFR to assess clearance), and ECG (lithium causes T-wave flattening/inversion and conduction abnormalities).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Serum sodium is worth checking (hyponatremia can increase lithium retention by causing compensatory renal reabsorption), but it is not the single best investigation for guiding management of confirmed lithium toxicity.
Correct. Repeat lithium level + renal function + ECG is the most complete and immediately actionable investigation set for lithium toxicity management.
Brain MRI is not indicated acutely in lithium toxicity. Neurological symptoms here are due to direct lithium neurotoxicity, not a structural brain lesion.
Thyroid function tests are important for long-term lithium monitoring (lithium causes hypothyroidism) but are not the immediate priority in acute toxicity.
Random blood glucose is not relevant to lithium toxicity management.
clinicalPearl: Lithium toxicity is increased by: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, low-sodium diet, dehydration. Therapeutic range: 0.4-1.3 mEq/L. Toxicity: > 1.5 mEq/L. Signs: coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, nausea (moderate); coma, seizures, cardiac arrest (severe).
keyClue: Stable lithium patient + starts NSAID + develops tremor/confusion + level 2.1 mEq/L = NSAID-induced lithium toxicity. Manage with lithium hold, IV fluids, renal monitoring.
commonExamTrap: Forgetting that NSAIDs (not just diuretics) cause lithium toxicity by reducing renal clearance. All NSAIDs including OTC ibuprofen are contraindicated with lithium.
missedConcept: Lithium is excreted exclusively by the kidneys and competes with sodium for reabsorption. Any state that reduces GFR (NSAID use, dehydration, ACE inhibitors) will raise lithium levels. Patient education about avoiding NSAIDs is essential.
redFlagReason: Lithium level 2.1 mEq/L with neurological symptoms (moderate-severe toxicity) can progress to seizures, cardiac arrhythmia, and death. Requires immediate lithium cessation and monitoring.
